result:
I keep thinking about the word “translation.” Not the mechanical act of swapping one language for another, but the quieter, more perilous translation that happens inside a single skull when experience rubs up against memory and both come out changed.

Last night I walked past a lit window and saw, framed inside it, a woman ironing a shirt. Nothing else—just the glide of metal across blue cotton, the hiss of invisible steam. In the three seconds it took to cross her field of light, my brain served up an entire childhood evening: my mother humming off-key to the BBC, the burnt-sugar smell of the old cloth cover on our ironing board, the way the wallpaper (brown leaves, orange flowers) bulged slightly near the ceiling where rain had once got in. None of that was in the window. The woman probably didn’t even own that wallpaper. Yet the sight of her translated me, ruthlessly, into someone I no longer am.

We like to say memories are stored, like winter blankets in cedar chests, but I think they’re more like water mains under a city street. You don’t know they’re there until the pavement cracks, and suddenly the simplest corner is a geyser. The iron, the blue shirt, the hiss—all jackhammers. Memory doesn’t ask permission; it just re-pipes you.

Sometimes I try to reverse the process, to translate memory back into fact, burn off the hallucinations. I call my mother: “Did we have an iron with a red handle?” She says, “We never owned an iron. I sent everything to the laundry.” The certainty in her voice is a wall I can’t push through. So either she’s wrong, or I’ve been carrying someone else’s childhood like a parasite. Both options feel like betrayal.

There’s a third possibility, the one I find hardest to forgive: that accuracy simply isn’t the point. Maybe memory is a translation engine whose chief product is mood, not data. The woman ironing her shirt last night gave me a mood—warm cotton, off-key hymn, late light on brown leaves—and that mood is true even if every pixel is false. The child I remember being is a fiction who behaves more like the real me than the documented child ever did. He is the better translator; he knows which pieces of the past I will need in order to keep breathing tomorrow.

I’ve been trying to write a poem about this, but every line lands like an apology. Apology to my mother for correcting her, to the stranger for kidnapping her silhouette, to myself for needing such elaborate scaffolding just to stand still. The poem keeps collapsing into a single couplet:

We are raised by ghosts who pinch our cheeks
and leave before we learn their real names.

I can’t decide if that’s bleak or comforting. Sometimes I mouth it like a lullaby, sometimes like evidence.

Tonight the city smells of struck matches and wet asphalt, the way it did the summer the power kept going out. I was thirteen, maybe fourteen, reading by candle stub, convinced the world was ending in style. Now the same smell arrives thirty years later, a telegram from a boy who no longer exists. He is warning me, kindly: Whatever you’re about to do next, do it fast; the iron is hot, the shirt is blue, the light is leaking from the window, and in a second the board will cool, the fabric will crease, and the mood will be gone.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
